1: 'Hear what YHWH says: Arise, plead your case before the mountains, and let the hills hear your voice.' Micah 6 opens the great covenant lawsuit (rib) of YHWH against Israel. The mountains and hills are summoned as cosmic witnesses — an ancient Near Eastern legal convention in which enduring natural features serve as witnesses to covenant agreements (cf. Josh 24:27; Deut 32:1; Isa 1:2). The genre is the rib pattern: plaintiff (YHWH), defendant (Israel), witnesses (creation), and charges.
2: 'Hear, you mountains, the indictment of YHWH, and you enduring foundations of the earth, for YHWH has an indictment against his people, and he will contend with Israel.' The 'enduring foundations' (mosadet aretz) are the permanent geological structures that predate Israel and will outlast it — reliable witnesses precisely because they cannot be bribed or intimidated. YHWH's indictment (rib) is legal: he is bringing suit against his covenant partner.
3: 'O my people, what have I done to you? How have I wearied you? Answer me.' The divine plaintiff questions the defendant with unexpected gentleness: not accusation but genuine inquiry. 'What have I done to you? How have I wearied you?' presupposes that Israel has some grievance. This is pastoral cross-examination, not thunder. YHWH opens by inviting Israel to articulate its complaint — an extraordinary gesture in the ancient Near Eastern context of divine overlords who did not typically seek consent from vassals.
4: 'For I brought you up from the land of Egypt and redeemed you from the house of slavery, and I sent before you Moses, Aaron, and Miriam.' The covenant resume: YHWH's saving acts that ground the covenant relationship. Exodus, redemption, leadership — the three leadership figures (Moses, Aaron, Miriam) reflect an inclusive tradition. Miriam's inclusion as co-leader alongside Moses and Aaron is notable and relatively rare in prophetic recitations of saving history (cf. Exod 15:20-21; Num 12).
5: 'O my people, remember what Balak king of Moab devised, and what Balaam the son of Beor answered him, and what happened from Shittim to Gilgal, that you may know the righteous acts of YHWH.' The Balaam episode (Num 22-24) is recalled: a pagan king hired a prophet to curse Israel; YHWH turned the curse to blessing. Shittim to Gilgal traces the final stages of the wilderness journey — the crossing of the Jordan (Josh 3-4). These specific saving acts are YHWH's evidence that he has been faithful. 'Righteous acts of YHWH' (tzidqot YHWH) — his faithfulness to covenant commitments.
6: 'With what shall I come before YHWH, and bow myself before God on high? Shall I come before him with burnt offerings, with calves a year old?' An anonymous voice responds to YHWH's covenant recitation with the worshiper's anxious question: what offering is sufficient? The escalating sacrifice sequence — calves, thousands of rams, oil, firstborn son — is designed to lead the audience to the reductio ad absurdum of ritual atonement logic. No offering, however extravagant, can substitute for covenant faithfulness.
7: 'Will YHWH be pleased with thousands of rams, with ten thousands of rivers of oil? Shall I give my firstborn for my transgression, the fruit of my body for the sin of my soul?' The rhetoric accelerates to the extreme: child sacrifice. This was practiced by some in the ancient Near East (including, the text indicates, by some Israelites — 2 Kgs 16:3; 21:6; Jer 7:31; 19:5). Micah does not accept it as a live option — the question leads to no. No quantity of sacrifice, however enormous, addresses the actual problem: justice, loyalty, humility.
8: 'He has told you, O man, what is good; and what does YHWH require of you but to do justice, and to love kindness, and to walk humbly with your God?' The most famous verse in Micah — and among the most quoted in the Hebrew Bible — crystallizes the prophetic critique. The answer to v. 6-7's liturgical anxiety is not a new or better sacrifice but three practices already commanded: mishpat (justice — in legal and social life), chesed (covenant faithfulness/kindness — loyalty in relationships), and hatzne'a lechet im-Elohecha (humble walking with God — personal relationship). These are not new revelations; 'he has told you' (higid lecha) — the Torah already communicated this. The problem is obedience, not information.
9: 'The voice of YHWH cries to the city — and it is sound wisdom to fear your name: “Hear of the rod and of him who appointed it!”' The city addressed is likely Jerusalem or Samaria. The 'rod' (shevet) is the instrument of divine punishment — the Assyrian army as YHWH's rod (Isa 10:5 uses the same metaphor). Sound wisdom (tushiyah) recognizes that fearing YHWH's name is the response to the prophetic warning. The call to hear is addressed to whoever has ears — the crowd assembled at the city gate.
10: 'Can I forget any longer the treasures of wickedness in the house of the wicked, and the scant measure that is accursed?' The rhetorical question implies the answer: YHWH cannot overlook the fraudulent commerce that permeates the city. The 'scant measure' (ephah razah, lean/short ephah) refers to the underweight measure used in retail trade — the dishonest merchant giving less than the customer paid for. This specific crime connects to Amos 8:5 (also condemning fraudulent measures) and Lev 19:35-36 (prohibiting dishonest weights).
11: 'Shall I acquit the man with wicked scales and with a bag of deceitful weights?' YHWH continues the court proceedings: the fraudulent scale and the deceitful weights bag are standard images of commercial dishonesty in the ancient Near East (cf. Prov 11:1; 16:11; 20:10, 23; Hos 12:7; Amos 8:5). The expected answer is no — YHWH will not acquit (ezke) commercial fraud. The scales of divine justice weigh accurately what the commercial scales of the corrupt city do not.
12: 'Your rich men are full of violence; your inhabitants speak lies, and their tongue is deceitful in their mouth.' The social pathology is comprehensive: the wealthy use violence to accumulate; the general population uses deception in speech. Both are covenant violations — violence against covenant (Exod 20:13; Lev 19:16) and lying against covenant (Exod 20:16; Lev 19:11). The corruption is not limited to the elite; it has permeated the entire speech-community.
13: 'Therefore I strike you with a grievous blow, making you desolate because of your sins.' The divine punishment is announced: a grievous blow (hechleiti hakkotcha). The root chalah (be sick/wounded) is used — the blow will wound gravely, perhaps mortally. This parallels 1:9 ('her wound is incurable'). The desolation (shemamah) is the result of the sin specifically, not arbitrary divine anger.
14: 'You shall eat, but not be satisfied, and there shall be hunger within you; you shall put away, but not save, and what you save I will give to the sword.' The covenant curses of Deut 28:38-44 echo throughout vv. 14-15: eating without being satisfied, putting away without saving, planting without harvesting. These are the precise reversal of the covenant blessings (Deut 28:1-14: full satisfaction, secure savings, abundant harvest). The text enacts the covenant curse structure without citing it directly.
15: 'You shall sow, but not reap; you shall tread olives, but not anoint yourselves with oil; you shall tread grapes, but not drink wine.' The agricultural futility curse (Deut 28:38-41) is spelled out for the three major Judean crops: grain, olives, grapes. The labor-without-benefit formula captures the essential character of the curse: not poverty but purposeless toil, effort that produces results which are then taken away. The triad of grain, oil, and wine appears throughout the Hebrew Bible as the symbol of the land's bounty (Deut 7:13; Joel 1:10; Hos 2:8).
16: 'For you have kept the statutes of Omri, and all the works of the house of Ahab; and you have walked in their counsels, that I may make you a desolation, and your inhabitants a hissing; so you shall bear the scorn of my people.' Omri (885–874 BCE) and Ahab (874–853 BCE) are the arch-villains of the Deuteronomic history — their dynasty introduced Baal worship systematically into the northern kingdom (1 Kgs 16:25-26; 21). Judah has now adopted the northern kingdom's worst religious and social practices. The condemnation brings the covenant lawsuit full circle: YHWH rehearsed his faithfulness (vv. 4-5); now the specific statute-violations explain why the curses are just.
